Client 2: And I’m Susan—yeah, I just want it to feel simple. Like, can we cover our spending and not get surprised.
Advisor: Totally. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow. James, Susan—what’s your household gross income per year right now, all sources combined?
Client 1: All-in, our household gross income is 94500 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what are your total monthly expenses these days—mortgage or rent, utilities, food, insurance, the usual?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—total monthly expenses are 3950 a month.

Client 1: All-in, our household gross income is 94500 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what are your total monthly expenses these days—mortgage or rent, utilities, food, insurance, the usual?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—total monthly expenses are 3950 a month.
Advisor: Okay. And any monthly debt payments—credit cards, car loans, student loans—anything like that?
Client 1: No, monthly debt payments total is 0. We don’t have any loans outstanding right now.

Client 2: And I’m Susan—yeah, I just want it to feel simple. Like, can we cover our spending and not get surprised.

Client 2: We track it pretty closely—total monthly expenses are 3950 a month.

Client 2: And I’m Client 2, yes.
Advisor: Great. Now let’s shift to what you’ve built up. Roughly, what are your investable assets total—so retirement accounts, brokerage, cash accounts, private investments—everything that’s not the house?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 627850.
Advisor: Thanks. Let’s break that down so I understand where it sits. James, do you have a taxable investment account—like a brokerage—held on a retirement platform?
Client 1: Yes, I do. It’s taxable, held on a retirement platform, and it’s valued at 252500.

Client 1: Yes, private markets held at a bank—value is 91450.
Advisor: Great. And then the home—just to capture it even though it’s not investable—your primary residence value, what do you use there?
Client 1: Primary residence value is 454950.
Advisor: And no mortgage or other loans against it, correct?
Client 2: Correct—no mortgage, no other loans.

Advisor: Okay. And any monthly debt payments—credit cards, car loans, student loans—anything like that?
Client 1: No, monthly debt payments total is 0. We don’t have any loans outstanding right now.
Advisor: Great—clean slate on debt. One sec, I’m just noting that down. Now I want to understand what makes up the income—what’s coming in and from where.
Advisor: James, do you have any pension income coming in monthly?
Client 1: Yes. I have a pension that’s paid monthly—annualized it’s 29250.

Client 2: No—number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And then risk comfort—when markets swing around, how would you describe your overall risk tolerance as a household?
Client 1: I’d call it moderate.
Advisor: Moderate—okay. Susan, does that feel right to you too, or would you say a little more conservative?
Client 2: Moderate is fair. I’m cautious, but I’m not, like, zero-risk.

Client 2: And I’m Susan—yeah, I just want it to feel simple. Like, can we cover our spending and not get surprised.
Advisor: Totally. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow. James, Susan—what’s your household gross income per year right now, all sources combined?
Client 1: All-in, our household gross income is 94500 a year.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And on the spending side, about what are your total monthly expenses these days—mortgage or rent, utilities, food, insurance, the usual?
Client 2: We track it pretty closely—total monthly expenses are 3950 a month.

Advisor: Hi James, hi Susan—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m James. We’re basically at that point where we want the numbers organized—income, what we’ve saved, and what retirement actually looks like.
Client 2: And I’m Susan—yeah, I just want it to feel simple. Like, can we cover our spending and not get surprised.

Advisor: Close, yeah—different from withholding. I mean your marginal bracket, the one your last dollars are taxed at. Do you know what that is for you two?
Client 1: Yes—37%.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And last admin-ish thing: when we work together, we’ll be doing this through my firm as an RIA—registered investment advisor. Is that the relationship you’re expecting, versus like a brokerage-only setup?
Client 2: Yes, that’s fine—RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Perfect. I’m going to stop there. Next meeting we’ll tie the income streams and the accounts you listed—plus the home and the zero debt—into a retirement paycheck plan that fits that 37% bracket.

Advisor: Hi James, hi Susan—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m James. We’re basically at that point where we want the numbers organized—income, what we’ve saved, and what retirement actually looks like.
Client 2: And I’m Susan—yeah, I just want it to feel simple. Like, can we cover our spending and not get surprised.
Advisor: Totally. Let’s start with the big picture cash flow. James, Susan—what’s your household gross income per year right now, all sources combined?
Client 1: All-in, our household gross income is 94500 a year.

Client 2: I always mix that up—like, is that the percentage that comes out of the paycheck?
Advisor: Close, yeah—different from withholding. I mean your marginal bracket, the one your last dollars are taxed at. Do you know what that is for you two?
Client 1: Yes—37%.
Advisor: Great, thank you. And last admin-ish thing: when we work together, we’ll be doing this through my firm as an RIA—registered investment advisor. Is that the relationship you’re expecting, versus like a brokerage-only setup?
Client 2: Yes, that’s fine—RIA (registered investment advisor).

Client 1: Sounds good.
Client 2: Works for me.
Advisor: Okay—last couple household basics, then I’ll let you two go. First, just confirming: James and Susan, are you legally married?
Client 1: Yes—we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And household size—just the two of you living in the household as adults, so two adults total?

Client 2: Operations.
Advisor: Got it. And income-wise for you, what’s your gross annual income—before taxes and anything withheld?
Client 2: It’s around forty-five thousand a year—hang on—gross annual income is 45250.
Advisor: Great, thank you. One sec—just noting that down.
Advisor: James, anything you’d add on Susan’s side—role, work area, or that income number—before I let you both go?
